speaker: interviewer
text: --is the 1st, of April, 1996. I'm Lorrin Brasg. I'm interviewing Rachel Hanan Hougnou. We are in Johannesburg, South Africa. This interview is being conducted in English. And this is tape two. Rachel, can you tell me what happened to the Jews, now that you were living in in these villages?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, as I say, we were in these villages. And we didn't even hear any bomb-- bombs anymore. And we were just even trying to, you know, to have a normal life again. And as we had a little bit of money, we didn't worry. We didn't go anymore to our houses. We left it everything there. But prior to this, you know, going to the villages, everybody in Rhodes Island, you know-- they had a little bit of gold hiding away for better days, you know, for rainy days. Because in those times, nobody believe in the banks-- you know. Now, you-- everybody, when you save a little bit of money, you put in the saving account. You do this and that. There, you know, they had-- they believe in buying coins, gold coins, little bit of jewelry. Even the poorest people, they had some gold things with them. So it means, you know, we all had buried in our houses very precious things. As a matter of fact, when later on, the Germans came and collected our jewelry-- I'll tell you just in between-- they never, in there, you know, their wild dream, expected to find so much precious things in a small island with a small population of not so rich people-- in Rhodes Island. Anyhow, we were in-- in-- in the villages. And on the 19 of July, 1944, you know, the Germans, they were send the two-- two SS from Athens, from the Gestapo, to come to Rhodes Island, and to liquidate the Jewish. There were two brothers in the-- who had-- Jewish. One, he was, Costa Ekinati. And he was like an interpreter who would speak very good German. And he came with two Jewish-- two German officers to Rhodes Island. And they went to the Juderia-- to the Jewish community. They find nobody. The-- the Juderia was empty, with not a single-- not a single person living there, except three old womans. One, she was a little simpleton, and then old man that he didn't know what's happening around the world. And even those people, you know, they were-- they're living with the charity of their neighbors. And they-- they survived the concentration camp, because they never knew anything more.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in any case, this-- this German officer went to the Jewish community. And they find empty. And they went to his house, which he lives-- this particular man lives outside the Juderia, in the-- in the smarts-- in the smart part of the Rhodes Island. And they ask, where all the Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they say, they are all, you know-- when I count up the bombs, they are in Trianda, Kremasto, and Villanova. They were three villages in Rhodes Island. Say, all right, you have to accompany us in the-- in our-- in our motor car, and come from house to house in-- in Trianda, [BACKGROUND NOISE] Kremasto, and Villanova, and advise them-- the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- the father of the household, to come forward to where we are-- to where we are, because, you know, it's nothing to be worry about. Just we want to take their names, and identity, and the general [INAUDIBLE], all this-- just for a census.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the poor men, all of them-- and they have to-- they have to go. And they go. And we left-- they left the women behind. And they went to the-- to the German quarters to put their names-- to put their names, and, you know, the generality, and everything. But they never suspected what was in store for them. As you can see now, just between-- like, you say, 50 years ago, how can you remember so many things-- the dates? But we stay, you know, all together, the lasted ditch horror thing for nearly a year. But in my mind, it's like it's been happen yesterday. I remember dates and names.

speaker: interviewee
text: But if you ask me for this something that happened last three years ago, I will not remember. Something that it remain forever in your-- in your system.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyhow, I was telling that. So they-- they came to our place-- tracked. They went in all the other houses. And we had three males in our household over the age of 16, which was my father and my two brothers. And they were taken, you know with the-- [? we did ?]-- they were not taken. They just went with their passport, and papers, and everything, to where their assign place.

speaker: interviewee
text: They went there. When they-- all the men there, under, you know, under their powers, they didn't let them come out. And they-- the next day, the president of the community came back again to our house to tell us, the men-- because he was forced to do so-- and in any case, they had the list of the community people. They cannot-- they cannot-- the congregation, they cannot deny. The next day, he came back again to our houses in Villago and those villages. And they told us that now the womens have to follow the men. And with us, we have to bring the provision that we had at home, because we were supposed to go to another village further down in the Rhodes Island. And if we had the means to survive, we survive. Because they were not prepared to feed us; and to bring all the money we had, and all the jewelry we had, in case we had-- we can-- they never thought we-- we had so much; and to come forward. Otherwise, if you don't compile with their-- comply their with their-- their-- their-- you know, their asking, they will shot the men that they were already there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, which woman will go and leave behind-- you know-- will hide and leave their husband, their sons, or their brothers in the powers of the Germans? We all went, voluntarily. So as I was saying, everybody is-- you know, buried their-- their treasure in their house. That same day, my mother send me to Rhodes Island, in the Juderia, though it was very, very dangerous to go. I knew exactly where all our things were. And I-- it-- it was not difficult to-- it was a plank under the window.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took it. And as a matter of fact, we had lots of jewelry there hiding. Beside all this from my mother, we had two old aunties who lived with us at the moment of the concentration camp, you know-- at the time of taking us. Their children were some in South America, some in North America.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were staying with us. And they giving, you know, for safekeeping, all the jewelry to my father, because he was their nephew. Well, the war was going to finish-- to send them to their children. So I went myself, and I took a bag, a very big bag, and I put it all my jewelry of my family. And I went back that night to-- to Kremasto, where we were staying. And the next day, we all went voluntarily where the men were. When we arrived there, we couldn't-- Did you take all your belongings with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were allowed to take the belonging that we can be, you know, shifted. But we had-- I-- in my-- you know, everybody had Persian carpets, and this and that. We left it in our houses. We couldn't take it with us. And we took it with us what it was, like, we can eat, you know, edible things. Like, if you had bread, or flour, or sugar-- extra, because it was during the war, which it was, many items were hard to find. So everybody, whatever they had, you know, edible, they took it with us. And we went where the men was. When we are-- they were there already all assembled. They were all sitting in the floor. And they told us, when we saw them, that they never been-- had anything to eat the whole night or to drink since the day before. And they were all in a bad, you know, morale. You know, they were very-- in very bad state. And then in any case, we stayed there for three days in that building, which previously had been occupied by the army for-- for the force-- how you call it-- the-- aerial, you know, forces. And then we stayed there for three days. And the Italians, the Italian population send food to feed us. They knew they were there. And then, many friends, you know, friends from one person to another person, they were coming every morning, even without, you know, giving their names or what even. They were depositing in the-- in the gate, big basket of fruits, big basket of-- even we didn't know who it was. They were just depositing them into-- to-- to be given to the prisoner there. They were very good. The Italians, they were very, very good to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, after three days, then they-- they-- they-- they make, you know, like the alarm, when the, you know, people, you know, they were having bombardment before. And after, they were-- they were given the siren, you know, to let know, you know, that there was something coming. They make it-- the siren blew.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody, the-- the Italian population and the Greek, whatever, they thought some bombs were coming, you know-- some airplanes were coming. Actually, they were Jewish to be evacuated to the port. They didn't want everybody-- when they, you know, usually, they heard that noise. They used to go to their shelter. But that particular day, it wasn't any bombs-- any, you know, allies, or whatever. It was the Jews. The Germans, they didn't want the-- the-- the population to see what was happen to the Jewish people. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any women go into hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not. As a matter of fact, as I was saying before, during, you know, the previous years, they was happened that three, four of our Jewish girl married, you know, out of faith. So when they heard, and they were, you know, protected for the time being by their husband, and their children, and this and that-- when they heard what was happening to the Jewish population, they left their husband, and they come and-- and joined the Jewish. Because they thought, you know, they made a mistake marrying, you know, out of faith. And they felt guilty, you know, that everybody was leaving the country, and they were going to be left behind. Like, you know. Like-- and they left. And they came. And all of them there, they-- they die in the concentration camp. They could have been saved-- because they didn't know that-- or suppose-- that we are going to be killed as well. But in any case, they didn't want to abandon their parents. They came with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any Jews remain behind in--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were left behind-- 20-- 20 or 25 Jews that live in the Juderia. Because some of them had Turkish passports, and then there was a-- a Turkish consulate in Rhodes Island who came and claimed them. You see? Because he had in his list the Turkish nationals. And then, there were a couple of-- one woman and another man, they were born in America. And they say, we were born in America. They didn't took them. And then-- and then-- that is 20, 25. Beside that, they-- a-- a young boy, a young man of 23, 24, he had a Greek girlfriend. And he hided-- the girl hide him in the village of Lindos-- it's the far-- the farther, you know, faraway village in Rhodes Island. And nobody knew that he was hiding there. Eventually, later on, when, you know, we left the island, the German, they find him there. And it was only one person. They could not make a ship or a train to send him to Auschwitz. They shoot him there and then. And they kill him in the island.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, what happened to all the Jews now who were gathered together?

speaker: interviewee
text: So, we-- we were-- as I said, they put it-- they put us in that-- these women in the boats. They put us in the three-- in three barges. It wasn't boat. They were barges, you know, like usually, they hauled 22 crew of people, you know? But now, we were 1,750 people. They squash all in these-- in these three barges, like 600 in each one. And at board-- in board, were only three or four Germans. And I must say, they were very good people, because they were not SS-- they were doing, you know, their military service. And they were ordered to carry those passengers from one place, that Rhodes Island, to Greece, where the main quarters were. But they were very good to us. They were very good to us in the sense, because as we ate with us some provision that we are taking, you know, when the German order us to surrender-- so they allowed to use the small kitchen in the boat, you know, to cook, if we have. And then, even their own tea, they were giving to us. And then it was July. July in the Dodecanese, in those islands there, is very, very hot. And then, we were not traveling at night. You know, there were, you know, the barges were stationary during the day. Because they didn't want the-- the ally to spot us. And then, you know, in the middle of July, if you were in those places, the sun is very, very hot. And it was very, you know, very uncomfortable. So those Germans officer, they allowed everybody, if you want, to bathe in the sea.

speaker: interviewer
text: We could have escaped. But where can you escape? But in any case, they were good to allowed us to-- to know, to-- to swim in the sea. How old were you at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: At this stage I was about 19. I was 19. And-- and I was-- I never dream what was in store to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They tell us that we are going to go to-- to Greece. When you were at the barges, you know, we are told, if, you know-- I suppose, that we knew that what it was in store for us, maybe we would have tried to escape. If we knew it was, you know, it was going to be the end of us, anybody, even the young people, they could have escaped or tried to do something-- even, you know, to put yourself in danger. So better, you know, to be killed to go all through all this-- all this horror. But we never knew. We never expected. We never saw any violence before in Rhodes Island. As I said, the Italians were very gentle people. And the Germans who came to, you know, to stay in the island during the war, they were always Wehrmacht-- Wehrmacht. You know, they were not SS. They start-- we start, you know, witnessing violence when we arrive, you know, in Athens, in Piraeus.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long was your journey from--

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- you know, the-- from Rhodes to Athens is a small distance. You know, on a clear day, you know, even Turkey, it is a very short distance. On a clear days, from Rhodes Island, you know, beach, you can see, you know, on a clear day, you can see some mountains of the Turkey and some even far away-- so-- distances. And so, actually, usually, you know, they take like, a-- like one day or two days to reach Rhodes Island to-- as this was short-- a short distance-- to-- to Greece. But that journey lasted eight days-- eight days. On the fact, you know, that during the day, we were stationary. In the night, only it was-- and then carrying all these people, it was heavy, I suppose, to go fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you arrive?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrive, you know, at Piraeus, which is the port of Greece. And that is where-- and you know, we saw the first atrocity and the first act of violence against the Jew. Even though, you know, those Germans who were in Rhodes Island, which took us in there-- in the-- in the boats, they were not so cruel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we arrive in Piraeus, you see-- we start witnessing the-- the-- the horror, and the violence, and the-- and the evil that they were. You know, because there we were put it in the-- in the-- in the lorries to transport us from Piraeus to Athens, in the-- to prisons, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, they were-- yeah, you know, to-- to-- to climb those passes. It was very high. And there were old people. And there were sick people. They could not climb fast enough. And they were behind us with whippers and hitting wherever they find it. And beside that, I-- I forgot to mention that when they come to take, you know, us from Rhodes Island, you know, they were in the villages. They went also to hospitals. I forgot that. They went to hospital. People, they were already dying. I had an aunt in Rhodes Island that she had a stroke, and she is at the end of her life. They took her as well to the boat. And eventually, she die, and-- because she-- she was unconscious. Eventually, she die. And they threw her overboard. And then, you know, there in-- in my street lives an old man who was, at the time, of the German, you know, came to Rhodes Island. He was in hospital. And he had his leg amputated. They went there that night. He had his leg amputated. They took him from the hospital, from there, with his leg and his blood coming out, you know, without even-- because he was in this-- in their theater. They took him out from the theater, with the leg 1/2 there, you know, hanging, whatever. They-- the leg was out. They took him into the boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Funny enough, this man survive the boat to-- you know, the trip on the boat; survived the train 16 days. And he arrive in Auschwitz to be cremated. But he was suffering. He was crying. They had-- nobody had any-- any medication to give to him. Imagine an old man of 80 with the leg cut off and the blood coming out? So when you arrived in Athens, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they took us to Haidari, which is the main prison of Athens, the Greek prison of Athens. We were there, you know. And then, the-- the old ladies, and you know, like my mothers, and all these, they thought, you know, they had premonition that something wrong was coming to us. And they start crying. They wouldn't eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as a matter of fact, the first day they give us nothing to eat. But the second day, they give us some soup and some stale bread, which we ate it. We were very hungry. And after three days, they took us to the rail-- you know, to the stations, and they put us in the-- into the wagons-- those famous trains, you know? Can I just take you back? How did you get to Athens from the port that you arrived at?

speaker: interviewee
text: With buses. With buses. They put us into the bus. That what I been telling you. When the old people were trying to climb not fast enough, these Germans they were behind us, and hitting, and doing. When they were-- as a matter of fact, I'll tell you that also. We were in Athens. They put out the men in one side and the women in another-- in another-- in another side. But they were-- as we were, you know, like 700 men, and whatever, so many women. They couldn't fit the 700-- the 700 men in one big hole. So they divided in two or three rooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now it-- but without food, without-- without any-- any water; so it happens, that in one room, where some men were, it was a tap with water. But in the next room, there were so many other men, but there was no water at all. And in that second room, there was a man who was diabetic. And he was very, very in coma. And he was asking for water. And then-- and then they start knocking at the door to the other hole, if any water they've got available there. They say, yes. We've got a tap. Do you-- there is water. But how can you give you a little bit of water for that man who's dying? So what they did-- unbelievable. So they look around, and they find like a small straw. So a man drank some water, and they put it through the straw. Another man, you know, they had another straw the other side, it was receiving a little bit, few, you know, drops of water, which they gave to the sick man, dying.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, that to say, we know how, you know, the-- when you are in danger, when something like that, you have to, you know, to help each other. Eventually, like I say, we stayed there three days. And they take us-- they took us to the trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this stage, did you know what was going to happen to you, or where--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because when we saw-- when we arrived to the train, we saw something, you know, evil was coming to us. They put us in those trains. There was no windows, or-- or-- or anything. There were no toilets. There were some trains that they were carry previously animals.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, and it was stinking. It was stinking, you know? And it was dirty. And there was no food in it. Only they put three barrels of rusty water inside to last us for 16 days. And then, they put us, you know, in each wagon 70 to 80 people stuck in those-- in those trains, you know? And there was a tiny little window, only to fresh air. And, of course, you know, during the night, many had-- even the day, many people were dying with that condition-- with no food. But we're still having a few things of our own, you know, munching.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after a few-- a few-- a day or two, people start dying. You know, 16 days-- impossible to survive without food, without water. And then in the morning, they were opening, you know, the-- the doors of the wagon. And they will take them out-- the dead people. And they will put it in the platform. There and then, they put gasoline and burned them there in the middle of nowhere. And we could witness ourselves. And then, you know, of course, you know, when you are there, you need also to go to the toilet. There was nothing you can do. At the beginning, the first day, we felt embarrassed. But after the second, third day, you have to do something, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- the men, they made like holes into the wood of the wagon. There were, you know, those wagons for animals, as I say. And it was all made in wood. They make little holes. And we were there in front of everybody-- no privacy, no-- no hygiene, no nothing. And we didn't wash, or we didn't-- we had no water to drink, never mind to wash. How many people were in a wagon?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've been telling you, of 70 or 80, at the time. You know? Even they-- even, you know, there was no enough room to be, you know, sitting. One-- many people has to stand. Many people have to-- to-- whatever position. You cannot even turn the other side. Because that small area, they cannot fit 80 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to all your belongings that you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was with us at the time. We still with us. But-- except, you know, I told you, in Rhodes Island, we had given away just the jewelry-- the jewelry. And they never-- you know, when they took it us-- so the jewelry in Rhodes Island-- when the German before, they took us to barges, they never expected us, I told you, to find so much-- so much precious thing in such a small island.

speaker: interviewer
text: During your 16-day train journey, did you at any time get off the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes, you know, for 10 minutes. And for 10 minutes, you can go in the fields. And the German was looking in case you want, you know, to do your-- your-- you know-- you want to do something. They were watching you into the fields next to the train. And then one day, I remember, because I felt, you know, very embarrass in front, if wants-- I wants to go, you know, to the toilet. And then, I felt very embarrassed that the German were watching me. I felt-- I went a little bit farther down into the-- into the grass. He came after me with a whip, and he whipped me very much so. And then my mother was there, and she was very upset watching me. And I said, don't worry. I'm not-- I'm not hurt. It doesn't make many means. So, but it was very painful. But just to not make her upset, it didn't hurt me so much. Don't worry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there soldiers in these wagons with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In the-- in the-- in the-- in-- how can-- in the-- a person live in that condition-- a proper soldier? No. There were-- every-- every-- every one wagon, there was a little compartment, a proper compartment, with two German soldiers. But not in the actual wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you speak about on this journey? I mean, what--

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole day, you know, everybody was fighting each other. You know, everybody was fighting. And they were very upset and crying that it was like, like you said there, when you see hell. It was something like hell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family all together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were all together.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you eventually stop? And where did you arrive?

speaker: interviewee
text: After 16 days, like you say, we left the 1st-- the 1st of August. We left Athens. And we arrive in Auschwitz, the 16 of August, 1944. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the first thing you saw when you walked off that train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when-- when we arrive, we saw that something-- it was nice. After, you know, being, you know, for 16 days in a train, one on top of the other, it was fresh air. And it was music. Actually, there was like a waltz-- the loudspeaker was playing waltzes. But what we didn't like, it was-- it was a row-- a row-- a big table-- a row of German officers sitting with chairs there-- about 10 to 12 officers. And in the middle of all these people was Mengele, that doctor they used to call the Angel of Death. He was there. And from far away, dressed as we were, and everything-- dressed as we were to-- with everything, he start, you know, selecting people-- which one to go to the right, which one to go to the left. We didn't know what it means to the right, to the left, to the right, to the left. Eventually, we knew the people who went to the right was to go to the concentrations camp, to Birkenau. Birkenau, it was the camp that we arrive. The one to the left were send-- they were waiting for them some lorries. And they took them away. And we say goodbye. And then we ask the prisoner that they were in charge, you know, translating the-- what it was-- you know, what it was happening. And they say, don't worry. They are old people. They are tired. They are going to be transported, you know, to their places, you know, where they are going to rest. They didn't want to upset us, because they were prisoner like us. And amongst them, there were people who was speak Spanish. They were from Salonika, and they were all taken before us. Actually, in Salonika, which was in Greece, there were, before the war, 40,000 Jews. And they came back about-- not even 100.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were some of your family in those lorries that-- that left? Were some of your family members--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in those lorries?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My mother. My mother, and my aunties, and my-- on the other side to-- like you say-- to go in the-- in the-- in the-- in the labor camps, was my father, and my two brothers, and my two sisters. And actually, all of them, you know, perish, except for my sister, who lives now in Capetown-- Mrs. Diamonte Franco-- and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you still have your belongings with you at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had it. But when we arrive in Auschwitz, those people who were speaking Spanish, I told you, from Salonika, and they were like interpreter. Maybe they put it on purpose knowing that we were Spanish-speaking people. They say, you must leave your belonging in the train. You must leave the-- the belonging in the train. And then, we came down without nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.
